question: What is the maximum allowed balance factor for any node in an AVL tree?
options: log n
explanation: Balance factors of -1, 0, or 1 are allowed. A balance factor of 2 or -2 triggers rotation.
reasoning_steps: Recall AVL balance factor definition
Identify allowed range

question: After inserting 3 into the AVL tree [10, 5, 15], what rotation is needed?
options: Left rotation
Right rotation
Left-Right rotation
Right-Left rotation
explanation: The tree is left-skewed: 10 has BF=2, 5 has BF=1 — LL case requiring single right rotation.
misconception_targeted: LL case requires right rotation, not left
reasoning_steps: Compute balance factors after insertion
Identify the case pattern (LL/RR/LR/RL)
Select the corresponding rotation

question: Why must every AVL node store or compute its height?
options: To display the tree structure to the user
To detect imbalance by comparing left and right subtree heights
To determine the parent node
Height is only needed for deletion, not insertion
explanation: Height enables O(1) balance factor computation: BF = height(left) - height(right). Without height, detecting imbalance would require O(n) traversal.
misconception_targeted: Height is decorative, not functional
reasoning_steps: Recognize balance factor definition
Understand why O(1) access to subtree heights is necessary
Connect to AVL efficiency guarantees

question: Predict the tree state after inserting 1 into this AVL tree: [10, 5] (root=10, left child=5).
options: [10, 5, 1] with a right rotation on 10
[10, 5, 1] no rotation needed
[5, 1, 10] after a right rotation on 10
[5, 1, 10] after a left rotation on 5
explanation: Insert 1 → tree is 10(5(1), null). BF of 10 = 2, BF of 5 = 1 → LL case. Right rotation on 10 makes 5 the root with children 1 and 10.
misconception_targeted: Rotations rearrange keys, not just pointers
reasoning_steps: Insert the value at correct BST position
Compute balance factors bottom-up
Identify rotation case
Apply rotation and produce new tree

question: A student says: 'Balance factor only needs to be checked at the root after insertion.' Why is this wrong?
options: Balance factor must be checked at every ancestor of the insertion point
Balance factor is checked only at leaves
The root is the only node that ever becomes imbalanced
It is actually correct — checking the root is sufficient
explanation: Insertion can imbalance any ancestor node along the insertion path. Each ancestor's balance factor changes by ±1, so all must be checked bottom-up until the root or a rebalanced node.
misconception_targeted: Only the root needs rebalancing check
reasoning_steps: Trace which nodes' heights change on insertion
Understand that imbalance propagates upward
Connect ancestor checking to correctness guarantee

question: Why does AVL use a strict balance factor of ±1 while Red-Black trees allow some imbalance?
options: AVL trees are older, so they use a simpler rule
Stricter balance gives faster lookups at the cost of more rotations on insert/delete
Red-Black trees cannot compute height efficiently
AVL trees only work with integer keys
explanation: AVL's stricter balance guarantees height ≤ 1.44 log n (vs RB's ≤ 2 log n), giving faster lookups. The trade-off is more rotations during writes. There is no free lunch in balancing.
misconception_targeted: All balanced trees have identical performance
reasoning_steps: Recall AVL and RB balance invariants
Derive height bounds from each invariant
Connect height bound to lookup vs write performance

question: Insert [20, 10, 30, 5, 15, 25, 35, 3] into an AVL tree step by step. Which insertion first triggers a rotation?
options: Inserting 5
Inserting 3
Inserting 15
Inserting 25
explanation: After inserting 20, 10, 30, 5, 15, 25, 35, then 3: node 10 has left height 2 (5→3) and right height 0 → BF=2. This is the first imbalance. 10(5(3), null) → LL case → right rotation on 10.
reasoning_steps: Insert each value maintaining BST order
Update heights at each step
Check balance factors of all ancestors
Identify first node with |BF| > 1

question: Consider this scenario: inserting a value into an AVL tree causes a Right-Left (RL) imbalance. What does this imply about the tree structure?
options: The imbalance is at a node whose right child has a left-heavy subtree
The imbalance is at a node whose left child has a right-heavy subtree
Two consecutive right rotations are needed
The tree was not a valid BST before insertion
explanation: RL case: node's BF = -2, right child's BF = +1. The right child is left-heavy. Fix: right rotation on right child (converting to RR), then left rotation on the original node.
misconception_targeted: Rotation cases are arbitrary names
reasoning_steps: Recognize RL means right child is left-heavy
First rotation makes the case into RR
Second rotation fixes the RR case

question: What color must the root of a Red-Black tree always be?
options: Red
Black
Either red or black
It depends on the parent
explanation: Property 2: the root is always black. This ensures that the maximum possible imbalance from root to leaf is bounded.
reasoning_steps: Recall the 5 RB properties
Identify the root color rule

question: Two consecutive red nodes appear during a Red-Black insertion. What does this mean?
options: This is always allowed for efficiency
A violation of Property 3 — must be fixed via recoloring and possibly rotation
The tree must be rebuilt from scratch
Only the lower red node should be turned black
explanation: Consecutive reds violate Property 3 (no red node has a red child). Fix depends on uncle color: red uncle → recolor, black uncle → rotate + recolor.
reasoning_steps: Recognize Property 3 violation
Check uncle color
Apply recolor or rotate strategy

question: In an RB tree, a black node has a red left child and we are inserting under the red child. The uncle is black. What happens?
options: Just recolor the parent and uncle to black, grandparent to red
Perform a rotation and recolor — the specific rotation depends on orientation
Ignore the violation — it resolves itself
Recolor the new node to black
explanation: When uncle is black, recoloring alone won't fix it. A rotation is needed to restructure, followed by recolor. The exact rotation (left/right) depends on whether the case is LL, LR, RL, or RR.
misconception_targeted: Recoloring always fixes RB violations
reasoning_steps: Check uncle color to determine strategy
Recognize recolor-only is insufficient when uncle is black
Identify the rotation case
Execute rotation then recolor

question: Why does a Red-Black tree allow a path to have twice as many black nodes as another path, while AVL does not allow such imbalance?
options: RB trees are less efficient than AVL trees
RB trees bound the black-height, not the total height — this reduces rotations while keeping O(log n)
AVL trees don't use colors so they are simpler
RB trees don't actually guarantee logarithmic height
explanation: RB trees guarantee black-height balance: every path has the same number of black nodes. The longest path (alternating red-black) is at most 2× the shortest (all black). This gives O(log n) height with fewer rotations than AVL.
misconception_targeted: RB trees are just AVL with colors
reasoning_steps: Understand black-height invariant
Derive that longest path ≤ 2 × shortest path
Connect to O(log n) bound
Compare rotation frequency with AVL

question: Predict the tree state after inserting 10, 5, 15 into an empty Red-Black tree:
options: Root 10 (black), 5 (red), 15 (red)
Root 10 (black), 5 (black), 15 (black)
Root 10 (red), 5 (black), 15 (black)
Root 5 (black), 10 (red), 15 (red)
explanation: Insert 10 → root (black). Insert 5 → 5 red, valid. Insert 15 → 15 red, valid. All properties satisfied: root black, no consecutive reds, all leaves have same black-height (1).
reasoning_steps: Insert root as black
Insert subsequent nodes as red
Check each RB property after each insertion
Verify no fix-up is needed

question: After inserting 3 into [10(root,b), 5(r)] in an RB tree, consecutive reds appear. The uncle (15) is null (considered black). What fix is needed?
options: Recolor: make 5 black, 10 red, 15 red — but 15 doesn't exist
Right rotation on 10, then recolor: 5 black, 10 red
Nothing — consecutive reds are sometimes allowed
Delete 3 and try a different insertion
explanation: Tree: 10(b) → 5(r) → 3(r). Uncle of 3 is null (black). This is a left-left case: right rotation on 10, then recolor 5→black, 10→red. Result: 5(b) → 3(r), 10(r).
misconception_targeted: Recoloring alone always fixes violations
reasoning_steps: Insert 3 as red, identify consecutive red violation
Check uncle color (null = black)
Recognize LL rotation case
Execute rotation then recolor

question: A student claims: 'If a Red-Black tree insertion causes consecutive reds with a red uncle, we should rotate first then recolor.' Why is this approach incorrect?
options: Rotating never helps fix consecutive reds
With a red uncle, recoloring first fixes the violation without restructuring — rotation would be unnecessary work
The approach is actually correct for all cases
Rotation should only be used for deletions, not insertions
explanation: Red uncle means the violation can be fixed locally by recoloring (parent+uncle→black, grandparent→red). Rotation is only needed when uncle is black. Rotating unnecessarily adds complexity and may create new violations.
misconception_targeted: Insert fix-up always requires rotation
reasoning_steps: Compare uncle=red vs uncle=black strategies
Understand why red uncle enables local fix
Trace the effect of unnecessary rotation
Connect to efficiency: recolor is O(1), rotation is O(1) too, but restructures tree

question: Why do RB Tree deletions potentially require more fix-up cases than insertions?
options: They don't — insert and delete fix-up are symmetric
Deleting a black node reduces black-height on one path, potentially violating Property 4
Deletions in any tree are always more complex than insertions
RB Tree delete fix-up is simpler because there are fewer cases
explanation: Deleting a black node reduces black-height along its path, violating Property 4 (all paths same black-height). Restoring this requires more cases (sibling color, sibling children colors) compared to insert, which only needs to fix consecutive reds.
misconception_targeted: Insert and delete fix-up are symmetric
reasoning_steps: Compare what invariants insert vs delete can violate
Understand why black deletion creates black-height deficit
Recognize that fixing black-height requires more cases locally

question: In a min-heap, where is the smallest element always located?
options: At a leaf
At the root
At the last position
At depth 2
explanation: The heap property ensures parent ≤ children recursively, so the minimum is always at the root.
reasoning_steps: Recall min-heap property definition
Apply recursively to conclude root is minimum

question: After extracting the min from a min-heap, what is the first step?
options: Sift-up the new root until heap property is restored
Replace the root with the last element, then sift-down
Rebuild the entire heap from scratch
Remove the root and promote the smallest child
explanation: Move the last element to root (maintaining complete tree structure), then sift-down to restore the heap property. This is O(log n).
misconception_targeted: Deleting the root means promoting children
reasoning_steps: Remove root element
Maintain complete tree by moving last element
Restore heap property via sift-down

question: When sifting down in a min-heap, how do you decide which child to swap with?
options: Swap with the left child if it is smaller than the parent
Swap with the smaller of the two children
Swap with both children if both are smaller than the parent
Swap with the right child if it is smaller than the parent
explanation: Swap with the smaller child so that the new parent is ≤ both children. Swapping with the larger child could still leave the parent larger than the other child, violating the heap property.
misconception_targeted: Any child smaller than parent works as swap target
reasoning_steps: Check both children against parent
Identify the smaller child
Swap parent with smaller child
Continue until parent ≤ both children

question: A student says: 'Building a heap from an unsorted array takes O(n log n) by inserting each element.' What is wrong with this reasoning?
options: Nothing — building a heap is O(n log n)
The heapify algorithm uses sift-down from the last internal node, which is O(n), not O(n log n)
Insertion into a heap is O(1), not O(log n)
Arrays cannot be turned into heaps without extra memory
explanation: Bottom-up heapify calls sift-down on the n/2 internal nodes. Most nodes are near the bottom, so most sift-downs are O(1). The total is O(n), derived from the sum of a geometric series. The top-down insertion approach would indeed be O(n log n).
misconception_targeted: Building a heap costs O(n log n)
reasoning_steps: Compare top-down insertion vs bottom-up heapify
Understand that heapify starts from the last internal node and sifts down
Recognize that most sift-downs are O(1), giving O(n) total

question: Visualize the array representation of a min-heap: [2, 5, 3, 8, 7, 6]. After inserting 1, what is the new array?
explanation: Append 1 at end → [2,5,3,8,7,6,1]. Sift-up: 1 at index 6, parent=3 at index 2, swap → [2,5,1,8,7,6,3]. 1 at index 2, parent=2 at index 0, swap → [1,5,2,8,7,6,3]. Done.
reasoning_steps: Append new value at end of array
Compare with parent, swap if smaller
Repeat until parent is smaller or root reached

question: Why must a heap be a complete binary tree?
options: Complete trees are the only type that can be stored efficiently in an array
The O(log n) height guarantee comes from the complete tree property
Both A and B are correct — completeness enables array storage and guarantees logarithmic height
Completeness is optional for heap correctness but improves performance
explanation: Completeness ensures two things: (1) array indexing formulas (2i+1, 2i+2, floor((i-1)/2)) work without gaps; (2) the tree height is exactly ⌊log n⌋, guaranteeing O(log n) operations.
misconception_targeted: Complete tree property is arbitrary
reasoning_steps: Understand complete tree definition: all levels filled left-to-right
Connect completeness to array index mapping
Connect completeness to height bound = ⌊log n⌋

question: What happens when a B-Tree node exceeds its maximum key capacity?
options: The tree is rebalanced from scratch
The node splits and the median key moves up
The overflow key is discarded
A new tree is created
explanation: The median key is promoted to the parent, and the node splits into two nodes each with floor((t-1)/2) keys.
reasoning_steps: Identify node overflow
Select median key
Split into two nodes and promote median

question: A B-Tree of order 5 (max 4 keys per node) has a root that just reached 4 keys. On the next insertion, what must happen?
options: The root splits, creating a new root with the median — tree height increases by 1
The new key replaces the smallest key in the root
The tree converts to a B+ Tree
The root merges with its largest child
explanation: Root split is the only way B-Tree height grows. The median key becomes the new root with two children. This is the defining characteristic: B-Trees grow upward, not downward.
misconception_targeted: Trees only grow at leaves
reasoning_steps: Recognize root is full (4 keys in order 5)
Insert triggers root split
Median promotes to new root
Height increases by 1

question: Why does a B-Tree maintain all leaves at the same depth?
options: It's coincidental — B-Trees don't guarantee this
The split-and-promote mechanism ensures every leaf path grows equally
Leaves are at different depths; the data is just linked for iteration
B-Trees require periodic full rebalancing to maintain leaf depth
explanation: Splits always promote the median upward, and merges always pull from siblings. This ensures every leaf path grows (on split) or shrinks (on merge) uniformly. All leaves remain at the same depth by construction.
misconception_targeted: B-Tree leaf balance is maintained by periodic rebalancing
reasoning_steps: Understand split propagates height increase upward
Understand merge propagates height decrease upward
Recognize that root split/merge is the only way height changes
Conclude all leaves stay at same depth

question: In a B-Tree of order 3 (max 2 keys), visualize the tree after inserting [10, 20, 30, 5] into an empty tree:
options: Root: [20] → children: [5, 10], [30]
Root: [10, 20, 30, 5] (single node)
Root: [10, 20] → children: [5], [30]
Root: [20] → children: [10], [30] with 5 inserted into [10]
explanation: 10 → [10]. 20 → [10,20]. 30 overflows: split at median 20. Root [20] with children [10], [30]. 5 inserts into [10] → [5,10]. Final: root[20] → [5,10], [30].
reasoning_steps: Insert keys in order, maintaining sorted order within nodes
When a node exceeds max keys, split at median
Promote median to parent
Create new root if parent is null

question: What is the key difference between B-Tree and B+ Tree leaf node structure?
options: B+ Tree leaves store all data keys; internal nodes only store routing keys
B-Tree leaves can be linked together; B+ Tree leaves cannot
B+ Tree leaves do not store keys, only pointers to records
There is no structural difference between B and B+ leaf nodes
explanation: In B+ Trees, internal nodes only contain routing keys (copies) to guide searches. All actual data keys reside only in leaf nodes, which are linked for efficient range traversal. B-Trees store all keys in every node.
misconception_targeted: B-Tree and B+ Tree are identical
reasoning_steps: Compare B-Tree key storage: keys in all nodes
Compare B+ Tree: internal nodes route, leaves hold all keys
Understand why B+ Tree enables efficient range queries

question: What is the height of a segment tree built on an array of n elements?
options: n
ceil(log2(n)) + 1
sqrt(n)
explanation: Segment tree recursively divides the array in half. Height = ceil(log2(n)) + 1 ≈ 4n nodes total.
reasoning_steps: Understand recursive division into halves
Count levels until leaf size = 1
Add 1 for the leaf level

question: Why does a segment tree range query only visit O(log n) nodes instead of all nodes in the range?
options: Because the tree is stored in an array so access is O(1)
Because each node covers a contiguous range — if a node's range is fully inside the query, we use its precomputed value without recursing deeper
It actually does visit all nodes, but it's still fast
Because only leaf nodes store values; internal nodes are for indexing only
explanation: Each node stores the aggregate (sum/min/max) of its entire range. If the query fully covers a node's range, we stop there and use the cached value. Only boundary nodes where the query partially overlaps need to recurse. This gives O(log n) per level.
misconception_targeted: Range query visits every element in the range
reasoning_steps: Understand each node covers a fixed contiguous range
Recognize fully-covered nodes can be used directly
Boundary nodes need recursion into children
Count visited nodes: 2 per level × log n levels

question: A segment tree stores sums. The array is [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]. Without building the full tree, which node values are used to compute range sum(2, 5) (0-indexed)?
options: Nodes covering [2,2], [3,3], [4,4], [5,5] (all four leaf nodes)
Node [2,3] and node [4,5]
Node [2,5] directly
explanation: Range [2,5]: Node [2,3] (sum=4+1=5) is fully covered. Node [4,5] (sum=5+9=14) is fully covered. Total = 19. Only 2 nodes visited instead of 4 leaves.
reasoning_steps: Visualize segment tree decomposition of [2,5]
Identify which node ranges are fully inside [2,5]
Use precomputed values from those nodes

question: A student implements range update on a segment tree by updating all O(n) leaf nodes in the range. Why is this approach incorrect for large arrays?
options: Segment trees cannot support range updates
Lazy propagation defers updates to child nodes until they are queried, making range update O(log n) instead of O(n)
Leaf nodes cannot be updated individually
Range updates should be done by rebuilding the tree
explanation: Lazy propagation: when a range update fully covers a node, we store the update in a 'lazy tag' and only push it to children when needed. This makes range update O(log n). Without it, updating a range of k elements requires O(k log n).
misconception_targeted: Range updates must touch every element
reasoning_steps: Understand the cost of naive leaf-by-leaf update
Learn lazy tag: store pending update at covering node
Push tag only when children are accessed
Analyze O(log n) bound with lazy propagation

question: In a segment tree with lazy propagation, when do we 'push' a lazy tag to children?
options: Immediately when the tag is created
Only when we need to visit a child node (during a query or deeper update)
At the end of all operations
Lazy tags are stored at leaves, not internal nodes
explanation: A lazy tag sits at a node until an operation needs to go deeper. The push happens on-demand: before recursing into children, we push the current node's tag. This ensures correctness while deferring work until necessary.
misconception_targeted: Lazy tags are pushed immediately
reasoning_steps: Lazy tag is stored when update covers full node
Tag remains until a query/update needs the children
Push transfers tag to children before recursing

question: A student has learned AVL trees and is now studying Red-Black trees. They claim: 'Rotations in RB trees work exactly like AVL rotations.' What nuance are they missing?
options: RB rotations are exactly the same — the student is correct
AVL rotations update heights, while RB rotations update colors and parent pointers differently
RB trees never use rotations — only recoloring
AVL rotations are left/right, but RB rotations are diagonal
explanation: The pointer restructuring during rotations is similar, but AVL rotations must update height attributes for balance factor computation. RB rotations must recolor nodes and maintain the RB invariants (especially black-height). The purpose and post-rotation invariants differ.
misconception_targeted: AVL and RB rotations are identical
reasoning_steps: Identify what AVL rotation must update (heights)
Identify what RB rotation must update (colors + black-height)
Compare the post-rotation invariants for each
Understand the same pointer mechanics serve different purposes

question: Why is the balance factor range in AVL (±1) a stricter condition than the Red-Black tree's invariants?
options: It isn't — RB trees are actually stricter
AVL bounds absolute height difference, while RB only constrains black-height
RB trees don't bound height at all
Both trees guarantee the same maximum height
explanation: AVL requires |height(left) - height(right)| ≤ 1 for every node, giving height ≤ 1.44 log n. RB only requires equal black-heights on all paths, giving height ≤ 2 log n. AVL is stricter, leading to faster lookups but more rotations.
misconception_targeted: All self-balancing trees are equally strict
reasoning_steps: Compare the invariants: AVL vs RB
Derive height bound from each invariant
Connect strictness to lookup vs insert/delete performance
Understand the engineering trade-off

question: A B-Tree of order 5 and an AVL tree both contain 1 million keys. Which will have fewer levels?
options: The AVL tree will have fewer levels (≤ 1.44 log₂(1M) ≈ 29)
The B-Tree will have fewer levels (log₅(1M) ≈ 9)
They will have approximately the same number of levels
It depends entirely on the insertion order
explanation: B-Tree nodes hold multiple keys. Height = log_t(n) where t is the minimum degree. For order 5 (t=3), height ≤ log₃(1M) ≈ 13 (internal nodes have 2-5 children). AVL height ≤ 1.44 log₂(n) ≈ 29. B-Trees are shallower due to high branching factors.
misconception_targeted: Binary trees are the most efficient structure
reasoning_steps: Compute AVL height bound: 1.44 log₂(n)
Compute B-Tree height bound: log_t(n)
Compare results for n=1M, t=3
Recognize B-Trees minimize disk seeks with high branching

question: All balanced trees (AVL, RB, B-Tree) guarantee O(log n) for insert, delete, and search. Is there any reason to choose one over the other?
options: No — the O(log n) bound means they are equivalent in practice
Yes: AVL is best for lookup-heavy workloads, RB for write-heavy workloads, B-Tree for disk-based storage
No — O(log n) is the same for any base, so performance is identical
Yes: RB trees are always the best choice
explanation: The hidden constants differ: AVL's stricter balance gives faster lookups but more rotations. RB's relaxed balance reduces write overhead. B-Tree's high branching factor minimizes disk I/O. The choice depends on workload and storage medium.
misconception_targeted: All O(log n) trees have identical performance
reasoning_steps: Recognize that O(log n) hides different constants
Connect deeper tree (RB) to fewer rotations but slower lookups
Connect high branching factor (B-Tree) to disk efficiency
Conclude: choose by workload characteristics

question: A student says: 'Tree rotations change which keys are in the tree.' What concept is the student failing to understand?
options: This statement is correct — rotations do change the set of keys
Rotations only restructure pointers; the in-order traversal and key set remain identical
Some rotations add keys, others remove them
Rotations only happen during deletion, not insertion
explanation: Rotations preserve BST ordering and the complete set of keys. They only change parent-child pointer relationships. The in-order traversal of both subtrees is identical before and after rotation. This is a fundamental invariant of all rotation-based trees.
misconception_targeted: Rotations rearrange the keys in the tree
reasoning_steps: Understand that rotation is a pointer manipulation
Verify in-order traversal before and after rotation
Confirm key set remains unchanged
Connect to BST invariant preservation

question: Why is recursion a natural fit for tree operations?
options: Trees are defined recursively: a tree is a root with zero or more subtrees
Recursion is faster than iteration for tree operations
All tree operations must be recursive — iterative approaches are impossible
Recursion is only used for tree traversal, not for insert/delete
explanation: The recursive definition of a tree directly mirrors recursive algorithms: if you can solve the problem for a node, and the subproblem for each child is the same problem on a smaller tree, recursion naturally follows. Iteration is possible but often requires explicit stacks.
misconception_targeted: Recursion is an implementation choice, not a structural match
reasoning_steps: Consider how a tree is defined: recursive structure
Map recursive definition to recursive algorithm
Compare with iterative approach (explicit stack)
Understand why the structure guides the solution pattern

question: In a B+ Tree, where are all the actual data keys stored?
options: Both internal nodes and leaf nodes store data keys
Only leaf nodes store data keys; internal nodes store routing keys
Only internal nodes store keys; leaves store pointers to records
Data keys are stored in a separate hash table
explanation: B+ Tree internal nodes only contain routing keys (copies) to guide searches. All actual data keys reside solely in leaf nodes. This is the fundamental difference from B-Trees, where every node stores actual keys.
concept: B+ Tree Leaf vs Internal Structure
misconception_targeted: B+ Tree internal nodes store the same data as B-Tree internal nodes
reasoning_steps: Understand B+ Tree architecture
Compare key storage: B-Tree vs B+ Tree
Recognize that leaves are the only source of truth

question: What advantage does the leaf linked list provide in a B+ Tree?
options: It allows internal nodes to bypass leaf nodes during search
It enables efficient range queries by traversing leaves sequentially without backtracking through internal nodes
It reduces the height of the tree by half
It eliminates the need for internal node splits
explanation: The leaf linked list connects adjacent leaf nodes. Once the start of a range is located via a search, the remaining keys in the range can be fetched by following the leaf pointers — no need to traverse up and down the tree. This makes range queries O(log n + k) instead of O(k log n).
reasoning_steps: Understand leaf node linkage
Compare range query with and without leaf links
Recognize the sequential access pattern

question: During a B+ Tree leaf split, what happens to the median key?
options: The median key is discarded
The median key stays in the left leaf node
The median key is copied up to the parent internal node
The median key is moved to a new leaf linked list
explanation: Unlike B-Tree where the median is promoted (removed from source), B+ Tree leaf splits COPY the median upward to the parent internal node. The median REMAINS in the right leaf node as well. This ensures all data keys stay in the leaves for complete range coverage.
misconception_targeted: Leaf splits in B+ Tree are identical to B-Tree splits
reasoning_steps: Identify overflow in leaf node
Select median key
Split keys into two leaves
Copy median to parent (key stays in leaf)

question: When a B+ Tree internal node splits, what happens to the median key compared to a leaf split?
options: Internal node split is identical to leaf split — median is copied up
Internal node split PROMOTES the median (removes it from the source node), unlike a leaf split which COPIES it up
Internal nodes never split in a B+ Tree — only leaves split
The median key is sent to the leaf level
explanation: The asymmetry is critical: leaf splits COPY the median upward (data stays in leaves), while internal node splits PROMOTE the median (removing it from the source). Internal nodes only contain routing keys, so the median is a pure separator — it doesn't need to stay in the source.
misconception_targeted: Internal node splits in B+ Tree remove the median key
reasoning_steps: Understand that internal nodes only route, leaves hold data
Leaf split: keep data complete by copying median up
Internal split: median is a pure separator, promote it out
Recognize the asymmetry in split behavior

question: Trace the B+ Tree insertion of [10, 20, 30, 5] into an empty tree of order 3 (max 2 keys per node). What is the final structure?
options: Root [20] with children [5,10] and [20,30]; leaves linked in order 5→10→20→30
Root [20] with children [10] and [30]; leaf 5 inserted into [10]
All keys in one root node [5,10,20,30]
Root [10,20] with children [5] and [30]; no leaf links
explanation: B+ Tree: Insert 10→[10]. 20→[10,20]. 30→leaf overflow, split: median 20 COPIED up. New root [20]. Leaves: [10], [20,30] linked. Insert 5→[5,10]. Final: root [20], internal [10]→[5,10] and [20,30] leaves, linked 5→10→20→30.
misconception_targeted: B+ Tree insertion follows the same algorithm as B-Tree insertion
reasoning_steps: Insert key into appropriate leaf node
If leaf overflows, split and copy median to parent
If parent overflows, split and promote median (removing it)
Update leaf linked list pointers after each split

question: In a B+ Tree deletion, when a leaf node underflows, what is the first recovery strategy attempted?
options: Immediately merge with a sibling leaf node
Try to borrow a key from a sibling leaf via the parent
Delete the entire leaf node and update the parent
Rebuild the tree from scratch
explanation: Borrowing (redistribution) is preferred over merging because it preserves the node count and only requires updating the parent's routing key. Merging is only attempted when neither sibling can spare a key. This minimizes structural changes.
misconception_targeted: B+ Tree deletion never requires leaf linkage updates
reasoning_steps: Check if underflow occurred after deletion
Attempt to borrow from left or right sibling first
If borrowing possible, update parent routing key
Only merge if both siblings are at minimum capacity

question: Why is a B+ Tree better suited than a B-Tree for database index range scans (e.g., 'SELECT * WHERE id BETWEEN 100 AND 200')?
options: B+ Trees are always faster than B-Trees for any query type
Leaf linked lists eliminate the need to traverse internal nodes for every key in the range after the first key is found
B-Trees cannot support range queries at all
B+ Trees store data in sorted order naturally without any tree structure
explanation: Once the start of the range (100) is located via a O(log n) search, B+ Tree leaf links allow fetching remaining keys (101-200) by following sibling pointers — O(1) per subsequent key. In B-Tree, each key access requires a full root-to-leaf traversal: O(log n) per key.
reasoning_steps: Understand B+ Tree leaf linkage
Compare range query cost in B-Tree vs B+ Tree
Connect to real-world database index implementation
Recognize why B+ Tree is the standard for DB indexes

question: A student says: 'B+ Tree and B-Tree are essentially the same — the + just means an optimization.' Which key structural difference disproves this?
options: B+ Tree internal nodes store no data pointers, only routing keys; all data is in linked leaves
The + indicates the tree uses more memory for caching
B-Tree cannot have more than 3 levels, B+ Tree can
B+ Tree requires 2× the order of B-Tree for the same performance
explanation: The structural difference is fundamental: B-Tree stores keys (and data pointers) in every node. B+ Tree stores ALL data in leaves (linked together), and internal nodes are pure routing structures. This changes insert, delete, search, and range query behavior entirely.
concept: B+ Tree Leaf vs Internal Structure
misconception_targeted: B-Tree and B+ Tree are nearly identical
reasoning_steps: Compare internal node content: data+keys (B) vs routing keys only (B+)
Compare leaf structure: standalone (B) vs linked list (B+)
Understand how these differences affect query performance
Recognize B+ Tree is the industry standard for database indexes

question: What guarantees that a B-Tree deletion can borrow a key from a sibling rather than merging?
options: The sibling must have more than the minimum number of keys (at least t keys)
Any sibling can always provide a key regardless of its state
Borrowing is always preferred but never guaranteed — merge may be necessary
The parent always holds a spare key for borrowing
explanation: Borrowing requires the sibling to have at least t keys (one more than minimum). The parent key between the two nodes moves down to the underflowing node, and the sibling's closest key moves up to the parent. This avoids a costly merge and maintains the node count.
reasoning_steps: Detect underflow after deletion
Check left sibling key count
If left sibling has extra key, borrow from it via parent
If not, check right sibling
If neither can spare, merge with a sibling

question: Trace the B-Tree deletion of 20 from root [20] with children [10,15] and [25,30] (order 3). What is the new tree?
options: Root [15,25] with children [10] and [30]
Root [25] with children [10,15] and [30]
Root [10,15,25,30] — single node after merge
Deletion is impossible — 20 is the root separator
explanation: Deleting 20 from root: 20 is an internal separator. Find predecessor: 15 (from left child's max). Swap 20 with 15. Delete 15 from leaf [10,15]. Leaf now has [10] — underflow (t=3, min=2). Borrow 25 from sibling via parent: parent key 25 moves down to leaf, sibling's 30 stays. Parent now [15,25]. Tree restructured correctly.
misconception_targeted: B-Tree deletion is straightforward with no complex cases
reasoning_steps: Find key in internal node
Replace with predecessor or successor from leaf
Remove the leaf key
Check for underflow and fix via borrow or merge

question: Why does increasing the order of a B-Tree reduce the tree height, but at some point offers diminishing returns?
options: Height = log_t(n), so doubling t halves log height initially, but log grows very slowly for large t
Higher order always linearly reduces height — there is no diminishing return
Order only affects leaf capacity, not internal nodes
Height is independent of order — it depends only on insertion order
explanation: B-Tree height ≈ log_{t}(n) where t is minimum degree. Going from t=3 to t=6 roughly halves the height. But from t=100 to t=200, the height reduction is marginal because log_{100}(n) and log_{200}(n) are very close. The practical trade-off is that larger nodes use more memory per access.
misconception_targeted: Higher order is always better
reasoning_steps: Recall B-Tree height formula: log_t(n)
Compute height for n=1M at t=3, t=6, t=100, t=200
Observe diminishing differences as t increases
Connect to trade-off: larger nodes = more data per disk page

question: Given a max-heap [50, 30, 40, 10, 20, 35], insert 45 and show the resulting array:
reasoning_steps: Append to end of array
Compare with parent and swap if larger
Repeat until parent is larger or root reached

question: What is the minimum array size needed to build a segment tree for an array of n elements?
options: n
2n
4n
explanation: Segment tree allocates 4n as a safe upper bound. The exact size is 2 * 2^{ceil(log2(n))} - 1, but 4n is simpler and covers the worst case when n is not a power of 2.
reasoning_steps: Understand segment tree as a complete binary tree
The height is ceil(log2(n)) + 1
The size is 2^{height+1} - 1 ≈ 4n

question: If you need to support range sum queries with point updates, which data structure is most appropriate?
options: AVL Tree — it maintains sorted order and supports range queries in O(log n)
Segment Tree — it stores precomputed aggregates for contiguous ranges, enabling O(log n) range queries and updates
B-Tree — its high branching factor makes range queries faster
Binary Heap — it can compute range sums using sift operations
explanation: Segment trees are designed for range queries on static arrays with point or range updates. They store aggregates (sum/min/max) for every contiguous range. AVL and B-Trees are ordered structures optimized for dynamic key lookups, not array range aggregation.
misconception_targeted: Any balanced tree can efficiently answer range sum queries
reasoning_steps: Identify the query type: range aggregate on array
Understand segment tree stores precomputed segments
Compare with BST: stores keys, not ranges
Match structure to query requirements

question: A database uses a B+ Tree for indexing. Why are B-Trees rarely used for this purpose despite being structurally similar?
options: B-Trees are slower for all operations compared to B+ Trees
B-Tree internal nodes store data pointers, reducing the branching factor and increasing tree height for the same page size
B-Trees cannot be stored on disk
B+ Trees require less memory because they don't store data in leaves
explanation: Database pages are fixed-size (e.g., 4KB). In a B-Tree, internal nodes store both keys AND data pointers, using more space per entry. In B+ Tree, internal nodes store ONLY routing keys, allowing more keys per page → higher branching factor → shorter tree → fewer disk seeks.
concept: B+ Tree Leaf vs Internal Structure
reasoning_steps: Understand fixed page size constraint
Compare entry size in B-Tree vs B+ Tree internal nodes
Compute branching factor for each given page size
Connect branching factor to tree height and disk seeks

question: Why does extracting the minimum from a min-heap take O(log n) while finding the minimum is O(1)?
options: Finding the minimum follows a direct pointer (root), but extraction requires replacing the root with the last element and sifting down
Extraction and find have the same cost — both are O(1)
Finding the minimum requires scanning all leaves, which is O(n)
The heap must be re-sorted after extraction, which takes O(n log n)
explanation: The minimum is always at the root (O(1) to find). Extracting it requires moving the last element to the root and sifting down to restore the heap property — each sift step compares with both children and swaps with the smaller, taking O(log n) time in the worst case.
misconception_targeted: Extraction and find have the same complexity
reasoning_steps: Locate minimum: root, O(1)
Remove root: O(1)
Replace with last element: O(1)
Restore heap property via sift-down: O(log n)

question: A Red-Black Tree and an AVL Tree both contain 100,000 elements. Which tree would perform better for an insert-intensive workload?
options: AVL Tree — stricter balance means faster inserts
Red-Black Tree — relaxed balance means fewer rotations per insert on average
Both have identical insert performance
Neither is suitable — B-Tree should be used for insert-intensive workloads
explanation: RB trees allow paths up to 2× the length of others, requiring only O(1) rotations per insert (amortized). AVL's stricter balance forces O(log n) rotations in the worst case per insert. For insert-heavy workloads, RB trees have lower write amplification.
misconception_targeted: AVL trees are better in all scenarios because they are more balanced
reasoning_steps: Compare AVL vs RB rotation frequency per insert
Understand O(log n) AVL rotations vs O(1) RB rotations amortized
Connect rotation count to write performance
Conclude: RB for write-heavy, AVL for read-heavy
